rmk:
Recorded in a forrest. Social calls are present between 4,300s-4,850s and 9,600s-9,750s in the recording.

*Nyctalus noctula* and *Nyctalus leisleri* are presumed to be present. The only reference material that shows a similar social call is a recording from *Nyctalus lasiopterus* - recorded by Alex Lefevre, figure 8.22.11 in the book "Bat calls of Britain and Europe" edited by Jon Russ.

Currently ID'ing this as leisler's bat, due to the similarity of the social calls with these recordings (that are verified to be Leisler's bat): XC822327, XC822328, XC822329, XC822333, XC822339, XC822412, XC822413
A prolonged feeding buzz.
Type D1 and D2 social calls of the Leisler's noctule. A type D social call of the common pipistrelle is also present.
A (feeding?)buzz followed by two type D1 social calls.
Lots of "buzzing".

Type C social calls of an advertising male Leisler's bat. In the short time between 3,600s and 4,200s in the recording, there is a common pipistrelle social call, Leisler's bat social call and brown long-eared bat social call present.
Type C social calls of an advertising male Leisler's bat.
Resident species in attic.

Two of our fieldworkers found a roost of the common noctule in a tree, assumed to be a mating roost. One bat was clearly visible sitting inside the tree hole, while it was emitting calls. This bat is assumed to be the male. The calls are very similar to the recordings of Jon Russ of a male noctule calling from a tree (Russ, 2021). A few encounters with a close-approaching bat, assumed to be female, took place. A big variety of social calls were recorded.

- Jon Russ (2021). Bat Calls of Britain and Europe: a Guide to Species Identification. Pelagic Publishing.

Old pineforest with a lot of really old trees and both standing dead trees and dead trunks on ground. The forest been controlled burned in late may 2013. During this work 2013, some bats were disturbed by the heat and smoke from the fire when we saw them flying around from a old pine with hollow.

Recorded in a around 130 years old pine dominated forest in a lower part of a mountain. There are good  connection with other areas with old forest and some nearby forest stands have elements of more older trees and standing dead trees. Close to the recording place are a woodland lake. 

From 10 of june to 18 of june there was around 600 registrations of E.nilssonii here.

rec: Sander Boersma, Sarah Mahie, Boaz van Die
rmk: Distinctive social calls from within the maternity roost (at least 30 individuals) of serotine bats.

The calls in this recording are from serotine bats that were emerging from the maternity roost where at least 30 individuals reside. At first I thought we were recording echolocation of long-eared bats, but it was clear from the video footage (and the time of emerging) that these were serotine bats. The specifications of the calls do not match with the specifications given in the book "Bat Calls of Britain and Europe" by Jon Russ. The minimum / end frequency of the calls are around 31 kHz and the peak frequency of the calls are around 35 kHz.

The recorder was placed on a wooden pole 2.3 m above the ground in the marsh surrounding the pond. The recording is dominated by type C arched social calls. FmaxE: 46.4 kHz; Duration: 7.7 ms; Time Between Calls: 91.8 ms.
Simple calculation of signal/noise ratio: dBmax - dBmin = -2.55 dB - (-52.45 dB) = 49.90 dB (= 50 dB i.e. quality A).
See additional information at: <https://www.artsobservasjoner.no/Sighting/37277394>

SNR 46dB(ISO/ITU) @ t/10 (i.e. downgraded to sound quality B).
A Daubenton's bat roosting in it's hibernation area (an underground bunker) was disturbed by the use of a flashlight and started to emit type C social calls.

A Daubenton's bat roosting in it's hibernation area (an underground bunker) was disturbed by the use of a flashlight and started to emit type C social calls. Later in the recording, echolocation of a flying Daubenton's bat is also present.

These type A and type C social calls were recorded next to a verified maternity roost where at least 54 adult Daubenton's bats reside.

An electric car drove by during the recording.
